q: What is an entity in data modeling?
a: An entity is a real-world thing you want to store data about, like a Customer, an Order, or a Product. In the model it usually becomes one table, and each row in that table is one of those things.
fs: An entity is a thing you track (Customer, Order); it maps to a table, and each row is one instance of it.
fj: Mixing up the entity (the type, like Customer) with a single record (one specific customer).

a: An attribute is a single piece of information about an entity. For a Customer, attributes might be name, email, and city. In a table, each attribute is one column.
fs: An attribute is one column on an entity: a named fact about it, like name or email.
fj: Thinking an attribute is a whole row; it is just one field (column), not the entire record.

a: A primary key is a column whose value is unique for every row, so it can point to exactly one record. It is how you tell two rows apart, even if all their other values look the same.
fs: A primary key uniquely identifies each row (e.g. customer_id); it is never duplicated or empty.
fj: Picking a non-unique column like name as the key; two people can share a name, so it cannot identify one row.

a: A foreign key is a column in one table that points to the primary key of another table. It is how two tables link together, like an Order storing the customer_id of the Customer who placed it.
fs: A foreign key is a column that references another table's primary key, connecting the two tables.
fj: Thinking a foreign key must be unique like a primary key; many orders can share one customer_id.

a: A relationship describes how two entities are connected. The most common is one-to-many: one Customer can have many Orders, but each Order belongs to just one Customer. The foreign key is what records that link.
fs: A relationship links entities; one-to-many means one parent row maps to many child rows via a foreign key.
fj: Assuming every relationship is one-to-one; most real links are one-to-many (one customer, many orders).
